speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 19, 1996. We are with Cela Sarna. This is tape two. Mrs. Sarna, we were talking about you working cleaning for the Germans and peeling potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Potatoes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you wanted to tell me a little bit about your religious upbringing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother was very, very religious because she came out from a very religious-- I don't think so my father was that religious, but my mother was very, very religious. And she really-- she waited in the morning before I went to school, I should say the [INAUDIBLE]. And at night I was too tired and I didn't-- and when I went to bed, sometimes I went to bed without saying [INAUDIBLE]. So she used to come over to the bed, she says to me, you didn't say the [INAUDIBLE]? And maybe-- maybe God won't give you life next morning. And I got up, I washed my hands, and I used to say [INAUDIBLE] not because I wanted, because I think the way she brought me up, all religious was on fear. I was afraid that I won't wake up, and I was afraid that I won't do good in school. And that's how I said the [INAUDIBLE] and that's how I said [INAUDIBLE]. And-- and Saturday, I wouldn't do anything bad because you know, she watched me. She watched over me. And my mother was really a very good mother. She used to that in the morning to all her-- my sisters and help them with the children and-- and then she came home. And I was really jealous that she is running to the married kids, and-- and she's not with me. So I used to say, mommy, when I grow up, I will have only one child, not eight. And I won't marry a man older than-- than our father. He will be in the same age. That reason I said it is because when Saturday came along, she wanted to go to visit all her-- all her daughters, her sisters and my father was tired from working a long week-- a long week. He used to say, go by yourself or wait later. And he fell asleep. That's the reason I said it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back, then, now to when the Germans came and you had to go to work every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I went to work everyday and sometimes we brought home in the pocket a few potatoes or we peeled the potatoes a little bit-- for potatoes, we really were afraid. So we'd peel the potato peels a little bit heavier, and we put it in the pocket and we brought it home and Mother used to cook. Or a little-- if the Germans left a piece of bread, we used to put it in the pocket and bring it home. That's how life went, the days went. We didn't know even what day it is. In the morning when knew it's morning because it's light and at night because it got dark, and we couldn't go out from the house anymore--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able--

speaker: interviewee
text: --because of curfew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to observe any holidays at that point or Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have nothing to observe. We didn't go to the shul because no shuls were open because all the shuls were taken away for the refugees. And-- and Saturday, we didn't have nothing to put on the table. The same thing what we had all week, nothing actually, we ate all-- we ate Saturday. So really, we became-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I tell you? We were not human anymore at all. We did what they-- what they told us to. And we were afraid. Everybody was afraid for the other person. And that's how life went. One day how we lived was a place to take water because we didn't have any kind of-- any water in the house. But it was like a-- with a hand you pushed it like-- Pump.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pump. Yeah, it was a pump. And we looked down the-- the window and we saw they took our rabbi. He was such a handsome guy. And he [INAUDIBLE] in a black-- black dress, black [INAUDIBLE], they called it. And they caught another German-- another Jew-- and they made the rabbi lay down under the water and the Jew pumped the water so long until he choked on it. And he-- he was dead. Came another crew where they took the dead away, and they took him away. This was this day where we lost everything like, everybody was afraid. Every second day, they used to come in to the Jewish section, and they wanted gold. They wanted diamonds. They wanted copper. And in order you should give them, they killed 10 and they laid them down the middle of the street. You should look at them. If you won't give it, you will die, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this, what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1940. There was shootings every day. We had one German, they called him the [GERMAN]. He used to come in with a dog. And he led the dog around and the dog bite the people and then, you know, the people fell so he finished them.

speaker: interviewer
text: He shot them?

speaker: interviewee
text: He shot them. It was horrible scenes every day. Sometimes people say, well, you weren't by yourself. But nobody knows what we went through. We were not even human anymore. And that's how life went. In 1941, they made like a pogrom. They went around the loudspeakers-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they told us all to go in the middle of the mark, they call it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That day they killed maybe, I would say 200 people. They went, they shoot whoever was too old to go. They shoot them. They told you to take whatever you can with you. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you take?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I take? A dress. You know what my mother took? My father bought from America two plush [GERMAN], two plush what you put on the bed, what you cover the bed-- covers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Covers. Blankets, comforters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was to put on the top.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, bedspread.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bedspread. So she took this and I took her clothes, a little bit, and my clothes. And I think she lost this on the way, anyway. Because when we came on the street, it was too heavy to carry and the Germans were shooting so she tore it down anyway. We never-- no, I don't think so she brought it to the place where we had to-- where we sat. She kept me by one hand-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the-- we went. There were thousands of people. Most of them, you know, people were from different towns what they brought already a night before. And we were sitting down, and they were shooting. My mother looked for the family. We couldn't find them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow we lost him. We went to look for the family and we lost them. We would sit there all day without water, without food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and at night-- evening, before night, when it started getting darker, they made like a line and we all walked to the station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were your-- the rest of your sisters--

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably sitting--

speaker: interviewer
text: And brother at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably sitting in another pile. And-- and we couldn't pick up ourselves. Soon you picked up, soon you got a little bit, they shoot-- shot you. They were from both sides. The Germans was hundreds of them. And soon you got up a little bit, they shoot you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your sisters gone to Russia yet at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I forgot to say-- to talk about it. I said that-- did I say that the Russian came in?

speaker: interviewer
text: At the beginning you said something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that and approximately what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was right away in '39, a month the war when came out. The two sisters, one with her husband and one by herself, they both went together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which two sisters was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was two sisters were they ahead of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Peskie and Itkie.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of things I left out. They went to Russia. And they really didn't go to Russia, they went to Poland when it belonged to-- to Russia. I'm going to go back a little bit. And it was very bad. They were refugees, too. So they didn't know what's waiting for them if they would come here. So they registered. One day the-- the-- the Russians said that you can register so there will-- so if you want to go back to Poland. And they both registered. And instead of taking them back, they took them to Siberia. And they spent all the wars-- all the time during the war in Siberia. They came after the war, 1945, the end of '45 they came back. Now I'll go back to the war. We were sitting in middle of this street. And when the evening came, they took us in a line to the-- to the trains, and they pushed us in the trains so that we could not even move around. There was hundreds of people in one train. The trains were not passenger trains, but the cattle trains. And already people started saying that soon they start moving, they'll jump because it's impossible. The older people right away fell, and they died because they didn't have any oxygen and from tiredness. I think that half [? of it ?] was dead by the time they reached the destination. They had to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what was in the train car? There were hundreds of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- yes-- I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there the sanitation, was there food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you kidding? There was nothing we didn't have a drop of water already for two days. I don't talk about food because nobody probably would have. But I think I pushed myself to a corner where-- where it was iron and the iron perspired. And I put my tankard and I drank the water from-- that dropped down from the iron. Then I said to my mother-- my mother stood by-- I said, you take it, too. Says no, you take it. And there we stayed all day there. I'll take a drink of water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the evening came, a little bit evening-- no, we stayed all day there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The train didn't move?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't move. The train stayed there all night on this place. And when-- the next morning when it started to dawn, it started coming in a little light, the train started moving. And people had knives they prepared it. They opened the door, not entirely, and they started jumping the train. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this when it first started moving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when it started moving in the morning. People started-- so the Germans were really then-- the first time they were in one-- they were in the beginning of the train in one-- one section and they started shooting. A lot of people were dead. So my mother started saying to me, you're gonna jump. You're gonna-- I said, where will I jump, Ma? She says, you're gonna jump.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll be my [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what [NON-ENGLISH] means? Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: That me-- you will be my survivor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- I-- and the older people said, if you jump, they said to each other, jump in the way the train goes because if you go the opposite, the wind will blow away and this way the wind will blow with you. And I saw a lot of people jumping. I didn't know if they survived or they didn't. And I saw a lot of people laying dead on the-- on the thing. That time I could breathe already. And I decided to jump. I kissed my mother goodbye and I jumped. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I jumped the train and I probably was unconscious. I don't know. But when I woke up, the sun was already out and I noticed I am in a field. I was in a ditch, really, and it was soft. But I must fell with the right-- the right knee on something and the right knee was hurting me. I could not move my knee. And I-- I figured, well, let me see if I can move myself and I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I look around and I saw some corn. And I figured, I'll go into the corn. In case the Germans come along, I'll at least lay in the corn. But first I started crying. I cried so hard. I said, what did I do? Why did I jump? Where-- where am I going? What will I do in here? Did you know where you were? Did you know where the train was going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody talk about where the train was going when you were getting on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: By then we realized that the train is going to a place where they're going to finish [? us. ?] The older people understood it. And they said, they don't take us in anyplace where they need us because otherwise, they wouldn't put us so close together that we could not breathe at all. And I was laying there and all of a sudden while I was laying came a farmer, a old man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was taking out the cows to the pasture. And he said to me, did you jump the train in Polish? I said, yes. He said, the Germans will go along right away of a patrol. Go into the-- to the corn. I said, that's what I wanted to do. He said-- and he said to me, what is wrong with your knee? I said, I must have fell.

speaker: interviewee
text: The knee was very black and blue. That's why I knew that I probably was unconscious a few hours. And he said to me, you see, there is a-- a place where I keep the cows. You go in there and I'll bring you some water. You'll put it on. But he told me right away, tomorrow morning you'll have to leave because I am afraid for my own life, and I'm afraid for my family and my wife won't know. He said, I don't see you going in there. You go by yourself. And all my [INAUDIBLE], really my foot was so swollen. I went into this place and there was some corn, too, inside. And I went in in the middle of the corn. He came in later and he brought me some water and a piece of [? rag ?] and a piece of bread. This was a luxury. I didn't see it already for a long time. I ate and I-- he even brought me a place to go to the bathroom. Majdanek [SIGHS] And I knew I have to go tomorrow morning. [? He'll leave ?] me. I really didn't trust him because a lot of people took you in and then they gave it over to the Germans. But I had no other alternative. I had no place to go, I didn't know where I am. I knew that I'm not very far from the town and I knew that there are people in the town. Because while we were sitting, the Germans came in with a list, and they called out people what they need, what they needed to work. And I think I heard that my-- my brother was named, too. [SIGHS] Anyway, I was by this man. He brought me at night some food-- not-- not cooked food because he was afraid for his family-- but he bought me in the pocket a piece of bread, and he bought some water for the cows so he gave me some water.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me he was gonna let me stay another day. He said, how is your foot? I said, it's much better because I tried to stand up, and I stood already on it. In trouble, it healed very fast. And he said, I have to take a [NONE-ENGLISH]. It means some things for the Germans. I found out that it's a lot of Jews and they made a ghetto. It's still in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll take you home halfway, and I'll let you off halfway out. The Jews are coming, and they are building a road. And then they are from [INAUDIBLE], and then they'll probably take you home. He took me early in the morning, it was maybe 4 o'clock, and he left me off on this place where he said the Jews are walking. But I thanked him very much. I kissed him, and he left me off. I didn't think he's telling the truth. I thought that he wants to get rid of me. But I have no other-- no other things to do. So I laid in this corn maybe till 8 o'clock in the morning and it came-- came a big truck with a lot of Jews and a lot of them I recognized. When it came 12 o'clock, they sat down and this one gentleman, the gentleman was the driver. And he was sitting all the time. During the day, he told them what to do, and it was very hot. So he sat in the inside. And when they had time for lunch, they gave them a piece of bread and a little bit of soup. And they were sitting, so I came out from the corn and I told him I'm alive. And they looked at me. I looked so beat up. I took off all my clothes, what I had on me-- I put on clothes one on under another-- because I was hot. This I took out-- took off right away in the-- in the-- in the train because it was too hot to sit so everything we-- we pulled off. And they said they gonna take me back to the ghetto-- that's a ghetto. At night, they made-- they made like-- they [? performed, ?] four of them, and I was in the middle. And they put me on this thing, and I went back to the town. This was very good. This was 80 kilometers from the town. I came in and my brother was there. And this was my niece, the one what she is alive. She and the old lady, the grandmother, because the little girl-- they had a little girl from two years old-- and they didn't think that she should go out in the middle of the town so they hide it in the attic and the Germans never came up and they survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the grandmother, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: The grandmother was my grandmother and his grandmother, too. I mean, his wife's grandmother. She was maybe, I would say, 85 years old or 83 years old. Maybe not even that because my mother married when she was 15, and she was her first child. So she was in the 80s, let's put it this way. And I came home and my brother was alive, too. For some reason, he escaped. He didn't jump the train, but he escaped and he was alive with his wife and the two children. They didn't go down, either. They were hidden where they lived. And so I lived in that my brother-in-law because he had my-- he had a daughter and a small child. We lived in the ghetto. And he was well off because he worked for the Germans and the Germans gave him some bread, and he used to bring it to us. But we didn't trust the Germans, so we knew we had to make a hiding place for ourselves and with did. The place where we-- this was already in the ghetto. And since he was a builder before the war, he was making wagons, [GERMAN]. So he knew very well how to do that. So at night we put a bed, and we took out everything from the bed, and we built underneath. We took out the-- the ground and we put some water there. And if we knew the Germans are coming, through the bed we went down and we were hidden there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did the Germans come around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very often. They came around very often. Every day they wanted something else. Because there were some people where they were rich and they pulled away some. They made fur coats and things like that, and they wanted them. So they came in, they shot 10. And-- and they didn't shot them, but they came and they said, if you won't give it to me, if you won't show it to me, we'll shoot you. And this was very often. Every-- every time they brought some other Jews into the ghetto from other towns and soon they brought them, we knew tomorrow will be another [GERMAN], because they bought them for that reason. This was it. So in the morning, they told us all to run out, to come out. Maybe three months later, we went into hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was maybe-- I don't know dates, really. I can't go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was probably in '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: The end of '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or maybe it was-- yeah, it was in the end of '42 or the beginning of '43. Every-- every so often. So they took those people we were again safe. And then three months later, [? there ?] it was again. In-- in May-- in May came my brother and he said, you know what? Tomorrow will be probably the last [GERMAN], because they didn't tell us anything, but they talk to each other that they have to get up 6 o'clock in the morning. Anyway, we hidded in this place. But they burned the ghetto. There was a big fire. So we all-- they knew that people are hiding-- so we all went to go come out from the-- from the hiding places. We all went-- this was the last one. This was May '43. And that time we went to Majdanek.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what happened. You had to come out of your hiding place.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came out to-- from the hiding place and they took us again. They took us to the same place. We were sat down and what wasn't that many people really like the first time. But it was because they brought all these new people from different towns.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people do you think there were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 1,000 this time. We sat again the same day, all day. And all-- by the evening, they took us to the-- to the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any belongings at that point to take with you? Did you have shoes, a coat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just what I wore. Just what I wore. No, I didn't wait-- take nothing. We were cold maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you been working all along?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was working for the Germans. They used to come into the ghetto and take us out in the morning and bring us back at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they taking you to work, what kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still the same thing, clean for the Germans in-- in the kitchen or in the kitchen where they gave us food, too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were sitting. We went into the-- to the train. The following morning, the train started going but this time nobody jumped. It wasn't-- it was almost like you were resignated, you didn't care anymore. You went through so much, and you can take so much. Nobody jumped the train. Maybe five hours later, we came to a camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came to a camp, so it was a selection. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The older people went in one side, the kids in another side, the middle-aged people came in another side, and the young kids came in another side, like in four-- in five places. My brother was in another place, my brother-in-law was-- men-- men-- men were separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: The men and women were separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Separated, yeah. Women were separated with the men. I never saw anybody again. We went with the young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where you were at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was written Majdanek concentration camp when we walked in. Majdanek, really not concentration camp, but Majdanek. And they took us. I didn't see anybody anymore, just with the young I went. There were kids I think from 13 till 20, not any older ones than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you just girls at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just girls. And they took us to a place where stood a lot of Germans. We went like in a alley.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a what?

speaker: interviewee
text: In an alley.

speaker: interviewer
text: In an alley?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were little rooms like you have for dogs or for-- for horses, little rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stalls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stalls, right. Just doesn't come in the right time. And they took us like 20 at the time, 20 in one time. And at every 21, it was a German at the beginning and a German at the end. And as we started walking, they started saying, dresses off, [? and we threw ?] the dress.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think we're going to stop now and change the tape and come back to it.
